content: Influenza is caused by orthomyxoviruses: Influenza A (subtypes based on hemagglutinin H1-H18 and neuraminidase N1-N11), Influenza B (Victoria and Yamagata lineages), Influenza C (mild disease), and Influenza D (cattle only). Antigenic drift: gradual accumulation of point mutations in HA/NA genes causing seasonal epidemics requiring annual vaccine updates. Antigenic shift: reassortment of gene segments between different influenza strains (often involving animal reservoirs) causing pandemics (e.g., 2009 H1N1). Transmission via respiratory droplets and fomites. Incubation 1-4 days. Symptoms: abrupt fever, myalgia, headache, cough, sore throat, fatigue. Complications: pneumonia (viral or bacterial superinfection), myocarditis, encephalitis, exacerbation of chronic conditions.

Antiviral treatment recommended for all hospitalized patients, severe/progressive illness, and high-risk outpatients (age ≥65, <2 years, pregnancy, chronic conditions, immunocompromise, BMI ≥40). Initiate within 48 hours of symptom onset (benefit still possible if started later in severe cases). Options: oseltamivir 75mg BID x5 days (oral, preferred for most); zanamivir 10mg BID x5 days (inhaled, avoid in asthma/COPD); peramivir 600mg IV single dose; baloxavir single dose (cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor, avoid with dairy/cations). Chemoprophylaxis: oseltamivir or zanamivir for post-exposure prophylaxis in high-risk individuals. Supportive care: antipyretics (avoid aspirin in children due to Reye syndrome), hydration. Antibiotics only for bacterial superinfection.

Influenza diagnosis: 1) Rapid influenza diagnostic tests (RIDT) detect viral antigens in 10-15 minutes but have variable sensitivity (50-70%); 2) Rapid molecular assays (NAAT) provide results in 15-30 minutes with high sensitivity/specificity; 3) RT-PCR (gold standard) from nasopharyngeal swab, nasal aspirate, or bronchoalveolar lavage. Testing indicated during influenza season for hospitalized patients, high-risk individuals, and when results affect management. Surveillance specimens track circulating strains for vaccine formulation. Vaccine effectiveness varies annually (40-60% typical) based on strain match; quadrivalent vaccines cover two A strains (H1N1, H3N2) and two B lineages. Annual vaccination recommended for all ≥6 months unless contraindicated.
